title: Authentication & Sessions
intro: How the application proves and maintains who a user is. Most breaches start at a weak auth boundary.

checklist.point: Hash passwords with a slow, salted KDF
checklist.details: Store passwords with bcrypt / scrypt / Argon2 (never MD5/SHA-1, never plaintext, never reversible encryption). Use per-user salts and a sane work factor.
Offer and, for admin/privileged roles, require a second factor (TOTP/WebAuthn). Treat SMS as a fallback, not the primary factor.
Set Secure + HttpOnly + SameSite on session cookies, rotate the session id on login/privilege change, and expire idle sessions. Provide a real server-side logout.

checklist.point: Rate-limit and lock out credential endpoints
checklist.details: Throttle login, password-reset, and token endpoints; add backoff/lockout to blunt brute force and credential stuffing. Log repeated failures.
Reject passwords found in breach corpora (e.g. a k-anonymity range query) at set/change time, and notify users of at-risk credentials.

title: Authorization & Access Control
intro: Being logged in is not the same as being allowed. Most access bugs are missing object-level checks.

checklist.details: Check authorization on the server for every action, not just identity. Default to deny; grant explicitly. Never rely on the UI hiding a button.
When an action references an object by id, verify the caller owns/may access THAT object. Don't trust ids from the client.

checklist.point: Least privilege for roles and tokens
checklist.details: Scope roles, API keys, and service accounts to the minimum they need. Separate read from write, and admin from user.

title: Secrets Management
intro: Credentials, keys, and tokens are the keys to the kingdom. They must never live in code or logs.

checklist.point: No secrets in source, config, or logs
checklist.details: Keep secrets out of the repo, client bundles, error messages, and logs. Use env vars or a secrets manager; reference, don't embed.
Give each service its own credentials, scope them narrowly, and rotate on a schedule and immediately on suspected exposure.

checklist.point: Scan history and CI for leaked secrets
checklist.details: Run a secret scanner over the repo (including git history) and in CI; revoke anything that ever landed in a commit.

title: Input, Output & Injection
intro: Treat all external input as hostile until proven otherwise; encode at every sink.

checklist.details: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements / safe ORM APIs — never build SQL, NoSQL, or shell commands by string concatenation with user input.

checklist.point: Encode output at the sink (no XSS)
checklist.details: Context-encode data when rendering (HTML/JS/URL/attribute). Prefer auto-escaping templates; set a Content-Security-Policy as defense in depth.
Validate shape, type, and size at the boundary; reject unexpected structure. Apply limits to prevent resource exhaustion.
When fetching user-supplied URLs, allowlist destinations, block internal ranges/metadata endpoints, and disable unexpected redirects.

title: Data Protection & Privacy
intro: Collect the least, protect what you keep, and let users exercise their rights. Privacy is an engineering constraint, not a footer link.

checklist.point: Minimize the personal data you collect
checklist.details: Only collect PII you have a concrete purpose for. Prefer not collecting, pseudonymizing, or aggregating over storing raw personal data.

checklist.point: Encrypt sensitive data in transit and at rest
checklist.details: TLS everywhere; encrypt sensitive fields/at-rest stores. Use vetted primitives — never home-rolled crypto.

checklist.point: Define retention and a real deletion path
checklist.details: Set retention windows and support access/export/erase requests — including backups, logs, caches, and third parties, not just the primary DB.

checklist.point: Keep PII out of logs, analytics, and prompts
checklist.details: Scrub personal data before it reaches logs, traces, error reporting, analytics, or LLM prompts. Review what third-party SDKs exfiltrate.

title: Dependencies & Supply Chain
intro: Most of your code is someone else's. A vulnerable or malicious dependency is your vulnerability.

checklist.details: Commit a lockfile so builds are reproducible. Avoid floating ranges for anything security-relevant.
Run an SCA / audit in CI and triage findings by reachability and severity (see the dependency-vulnerability-remediation skill).
Check maintenance, popularity, permissions, and install scripts before adding a package. Fewer, well-maintained deps beat many unvetted ones.
Use integrity hashes / signatures and, where available, provenance attestations so a tampered artifact is refused.

title: Infrastructure & Deployment
intro: A secure app on insecure infrastructure is still insecure.

checklist.details: Enforce HTTPS/HSTS, modern TLS, and security headers (CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, frame ancestors). Redirect HTTP to HTTPS.

checklist.point: Don't run as root; drop capabilities
checklist.details: Run services as a non-root user with the minimum capabilities/permissions. Use read-only filesystems where possible.

checklist.point: Fail safe and have a rollback path
checklist.details: Default configs and feature flags should fail closed. Every deploy should be reversible (forward/compensating migrations, not history edits).

title: Logging, Monitoring & Response
intro: You can't respond to what you can't see — but logs must not become a liability.

checklist.details: Record auth events, access-control denials, and admin actions with enough context to investigate — and nothing sensitive (no secrets/PII).

checklist.point: Alert on anomalies and have an IR plan
checklist.details: Alert on spikes in failures/denials and have a written incident-response path (see the incident-response skill) before you need it.
